data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a cascading failure of interdependent palatial redistribution networks triggered by a prolonged drought that exposed the brittleness of their specialized agricultural and administrative systems.", "mechanism": "Drought reduced agricultural yields, starving the palace storage and rationing systems. As each palace hoarded or failed to deliver reciprocal goods, the tightly coupled trade and diplomatic network unraveled: ships stopped sailing, scribes stopped writing, and dependent specialists (soldiers, artisans) defected or rebelled, creating a domino effect across the Eastern Mediterranean.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence of sustained, region-wide above-average rainfall during the 13th-12th centuries BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence that palatial storage facilities were full and trade continued uninterrupted through the collapse horizon", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because the Nile's annual flood was buffered against Mediterranean rainfall patterns, and its more centralized, non-palatial redistribution system could absorb the shock without systemic failure."}
